questions.question: Who is the author of 'Three Days to See' and what is her disability?
questions.answer: The author is Helen Keller. She was both blind and deaf, meaning she could not see or hear, yet she learned to communicate and appreciate the world deeply.
answer_outline: Explain her double disability (blind and deaf)
Highlight her ability to appreciate life

questions.question: If Helen Keller were given sight for three days, what would she do on the first day?
questions.answer: On the first day, she would want to look into the faces of her friends and teachers, especially Mrs. Anne Sullivan Macy, who opened the world of knowledge to her.

questions.question: What would Helen Keller look at in the woods on her first day?
questions.answer: She would take a long walk in the woods to see the beauties of nature, such as the colors of the leaves, the trees, and the small flowers she usually only touches.

questions.question: What is the author's plan for the second day of sight?
questions.answer: On the second day, she would spend her time at museums and art galleries to see the history of the world and the beautiful things humans have created.

questions.question: How would Helen Keller spend the evening of her second day?
questions.answer: She would spend the evening at a theater or a movie to see the movements and expressions of actors performing a play.

questions.question: What would Helen Keller do on her third and final day of sight?
questions.answer: On the third day, she would go to the city (New York) to see the busy life of people, the tall buildings, and the everyday work of the world.

questions.question: Why does Helen Keller want to see the sunset at the end of each day?
questions.answer: She wants to see the sunset to witness the magnificent colors and the transition from day to night, which she has only ever felt as a change in temperature.

questions.question: What advice does Helen Keller give to those who can see?
questions.answer: She tells people to 'use your eyes as if tomorrow you would be stricken blind.' She wants people to appreciate their senses every day.
answer_outline: Warning against taking sight for granted

questions.question: What does Helen mean by 'the seeing see little'?
questions.answer: She means that people with healthy eyes often ignore the beauty around them because they are used to it and don't pay attention to details.
answer_outline: Lack of observation in healthy people

questions.question: How does Helen Keller currently 'see' the world without eyes?
questions.answer: She uses her sense of touch to feel the texture of flowers and the skin of her friends, and her sense of smell to enjoy the garden.

questions.question: Why does she want to see the face of a baby?
questions.answer: She wants to see a baby's face to see the 'innocent, eager beauty' that exists before a person has experienced the struggles of life.

questions.question: What is the importance of Anne Sullivan in Helen's life?
questions.answer: Anne Sullivan was Helen's teacher who taught her how to communicate. Helen wants to see her face more than anything to see the kindness in her eyes.

questions.question: Describe Helen's interest in the city streets of New York.
questions.answer: She is interested in seeing the mixture of joy and sorrow on the faces of people walking in the streets, showing the reality of human life.

questions.question: What does the author say about the sense of hearing?
questions.answer: Just like sight, she says people should listen to the song of birds and the music of an orchestra as if they would be deaf tomorrow.

questions.question: How does Helen's writing make us feel about our own sight?
questions.answer: Her writing makes us feel grateful for our sight and helps us realize that we should look at the world more carefully and with more love.

questions.question: What would Helen see in her own home first?
questions.answer: She would want to see the small things that make a home, like the rugs, the pictures on the walls, and the familiar objects she touches every day.

questions.question: Why does she call sight the 'most delightful' of all senses?
questions.answer: She calls it delightful because it can capture the whole world, including colors, distances, and expressions, all in a single moment.

questions.question: What would Helen do on her last night of sight?
questions.answer: On her last night, she would go to a theater again to see a funny play, so she could go back into the darkness with the memory of human laughter.

questions.question: What does Helen think about the beauty of art?
questions.answer: She thinks art is a way for the soul of one person to speak to another. She wants to see paintings to understand the colors that artists use.

questions.question: What is the main message of 'Three Days to See'?
questions.answer: The main message is that we should appreciate the blessings we have, like sight and hearing, and use them to find beauty in every moment of our lives.

questions.question: How does Helen describe her 'dark world'?
questions.answer: She describes it as a place where she uses her imagination to build pictures of things based on what she can touch and smell.

questions.question: What did Helen's friend see in the woods, and why did it surprise Helen?
questions.answer: Her friend said she saw 'nothing in particular.' This surprised Helen because she felt there was so much to see that 'nothing' was an impossible answer.

questions.question: What would Helen want to see in a museum of natural history?
questions.answer: She would want to see the animals and the skeletons of dinosaurs to see the great scale and history of life on Earth.

questions.question: Why is the title 'Three Days to See' effective?
questions.answer: The title is effective because it creates a sense of time and urgency, making us think about what we would value if our time were limited.

questions.question: Summarize the essay 'Three Days to See'.
questions.answer: It is a moving essay where Helen Keller, who is blind, describes how she would spend three days if she could see. She emphasizes the importance of truly seeing and appreciating the beauty of the world around us.
